author: @everestneverest9720
text:
odd, i didn't see this in the playlists,it just showed up on the right recommended list,ohh well, a question robert, which magnetic/conductive ink formulation would soak into generic strong nylon sewing thread (would it stiffen it up?) for use in these paper based and related circuits for magnetic paper to paper batteries contacts encased in hot glue for longer use stability perhaps if needed and replacing the copper wire as in this use case.

the fact being, i'm not a fan in 2019 of adding non active inert material  in constructing a device,making it heaver for no real gain, just for ease and initial quarterly profits after commercialisation.

  don't use metal  for the case when you can make an extra solid battery in the shape of the case as it where, snap together magnetically  series/parallel connected supercap  backplates with battery molded front facia  with a thin  paper battery/supercap pcb controller slipped in place should be doable now if some electronics major can be bothered to translate the current existing circuits into paper circuits etc.

author: @caleb-hill
text: What's the strongest binding agent  for metal for a graphene based Cold solder "glue"

author: @enriqueomar1971
text: In experiments conducted by the Northwestern team, scientists try previously graphene, massively producing it without it losing conductivity. Using ethanol and ethyl cellulose is exfoliated graphite at room temperature, resulting in sheets one atom thick. This achieves a powder concentrate that is mixed with a solvent to yield ink.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: connect from pin 3 on the 555 through a 0.1 microfarad cap to a 10k pot. ground the other side of the pot and wire the central pin to pin 3 of an LM386. The stylus also connects to pin 3.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Yep - you would have to adjust the length of the resistive bit though

author: @Kokiri971
text: Would this work fine with your copper-based ink as well?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Lol. No I'm afraid not. It will draw with a dip pen but that's it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: If I had the money to buy one sure.

author: @davecc0000
text:
Like the content of you vids.

But I find the audio difficult to understand (no, not the accent!): too much echo due to room acoustics. 

Might you consider using a lapel mic?

Cheers.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I have been chucking it around for a couple of days now and it seems to be doing fine. Though if I wanted it more permanent I would use a tougher substrate and put a spot of glue on like you suggest - in fact I like that idea and will steal it lol

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Well I made this ink for electroplating organic substrates - so the ink is up to being plated. The main problem with making diodes out of it is oh mic contact with the semi conductor material. But I really like the idea. Then all printed electronics!

author: @MathewTitus
text: Exactly! Plastic with non water soluble ink - copper plated! Who knows with the right doping (different metals-semicondictors) you could make diodes and transistors too.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's a good idea. Maybe painting it on plastic?

author: @MathewTitus
text: Good stuff here! Have you thought of electroplating the ink to increase the conductivity? Might reduce the size of the circuit, perhaps. (Just thinking) ;)
